Event: Helps Jack save a man's life from the plane wreckage
Explanation: Risks himself in chaotic crash aftermath to help Jack rescue another survivor.

Event: Smiles and embraces the rain instead of seeking shelter
Explanation: Chooses to calmly enjoy the storm, gaining personal peace without affecting others.

Event: Boards the plane for a survivalist adventure despite handicap and brings a bag of knives
Explanation: Pursues his own adventurous goal and tools, which later unnerve others and initially raise suspicion.

Event: Uses his knives to hunt and butcher wild boar for the group
Explanation: Uses his skills and weapons to secure food, benefiting himself and the camp.

Event: Talks Walt, a grieving child, with deadpan snark about having a bad month
Explanation: Uses dry humor to acknowledge Walt’s loss in a grounding, mildly supportive way.

Event: Saves Sawyer and Juliet from being maimed by 1950s Others
Explanation: Intervenes as a time-traveling “hero,” clearly protecting others while also preserving his allies.

Event: Presses the button in the hatch every 108 minutes when he believes it will save the world
Explanation: Commits to tedious, stressful duty he thinks prevents catastrophe, with no sign of self-serving motive.

Event: Stops pressing the button, causing the hatch’s catastrophic failure
Explanation: Abandons his responsibility out of shaken faith, nearly destroying everyone including himself.

Event: Destroys the submarine that could let people leave the Island
Explanation: Eliminates others’ chance to escape to protect his own belief in destiny, hurting them and ultimately himself.

Event: Kills Naomi
Explanation: Kills an ostensibly helpful outsider to serve his Island agenda, alienating allies and bringing no clear gain.

Event: Uses knife-throwing skill years later to kill two people
Explanation: Deploys honed skill lethally against opponents, gaining tactical advantage at others’ fatal expense.

Event: Is beaten, shot, shoved out an 8th story window, and hit by a car
Explanation: Suffers repeated extreme harms that don’t clearly benefit anyone else overall.

Event: Prepares to commit suicide in his motel room
Explanation: Attempts to end his own life in despair, relieving others of his influence but massively harming himself.

Event: Ben talks Locke out of suicide to get information
Explanation: Temporarily preserves Locke to extract intel, helping himself while only ambiguously helping Locke.

Event: Ben strangles Locke and stages it as suicide
Explanation: Eliminates Locke out of jealousy and uses his death to manipulate others, gravely harming Locke.

Event: Uses an aerosol flamethrower to fight off a polar bear
Explanation: Improvises a weapon to fend off a deadly threat, protecting himself and others.

Event: Uses his crutches to stop “Henry” from strangling Ana Lucia
Explanation: Uses mobility aid as a weapon to interrupt a murder attempt, with clear benefit to the victim.

Event: Tries to stop Jack from contacting the Freighter at gunpoint with an (apparently) empty gun
Explanation: Uses coercion and apparent lethal threat to enforce his Island agenda at Jack’s expense.

Event: Kills people “for the island” at the height of his fanaticism
Explanation: Commits murder under zealotry, sacrificing others for a cause that only questionably serves him.

Event: Lies wounded in a mass grave and decides to shoot himself
Explanation: Attempts suicide to escape suffering, ending his own potential while barely affecting others.

Event: Heeds a vision of Walt and decides not to shoot himself
Explanation: Rejects suicide, regaining will to live and preserving his later positive impact on others.

Event: Fails to convince any of the Oceanic Six to return to the Island
Explanation: His efforts fail, leaving him demoralized and not helping them in the short term.

Event: Decides to hang himself after failing to recruit the Oceanic Six
Explanation: Chooses self-destruction out of despair, harming himself while offering only dubious “relief” to others.

Event: Jack, believing Locke’s suicide, is emotionally broken and later honors Locke’s memory
Explanation: Transforms his guilt into renewed commitment to Locke’s cause, aiding Island and survivors.

Event: Fights to survive after being born three months early
Explanation: Instinctively clings to life against heavy odds, benefiting himself and inspiring others.

Event: Works to open the hatch with relentless determination
Explanation: Pursues a risky, grueling goal to unlock Island secrets and a potential shelter/resource.

Event: Pushes himself to keep pressing the button as a duty
Explanation: Accepts ongoing burden and stress for presumed collective safety.

Event: Accepts total blame for destruction of the hatch
Explanation: Internalizes responsibility, psychologically harming himself while clarifying accountability for others.

Event: Is devastated by Boone’s death and briefly loses his faith
Explanation: Emotionally collapses under guilt, hindering his own effectiveness without helping anyone.

Event: Reframes Boone’s death as “a sacrifice the Island demanded”
Explanation: Uses spiritual rationalization to ease his guilt, devaluing Boone’s life for his own psychological comfort.

Event: Later admits remorse about Boone and shows shame over his earlier rationalization
Explanation: Re-embraces honest guilt, regaining moral clarity and respect for Boone’s sacrifice.

Event: Helps Walt learn knife-throwing behind Michael’s back
Explanation: Fosters Walt’s interest and confidence but undermines Michael’s parental authority.

Event: Respects Michael’s demand to stay away from Walt
Explanation: Honors the father’s request despite disagreement, stabilizing the parent-child relationship.

Event: Refuses to kill Anthony Cooper despite having every reason
Explanation: Spurns vengeance at personal psychological cost, sparing his abusive father.

Event: Arranges for Sawyer to kill Anthony Cooper instead of doing it himself
Explanation: Shifts the burden of murder onto Sawyer to resolve his own problem, harming Cooper and implicating Sawyer.

Event: Honors Jin’s request not to bring Sun back to the Island
Explanation: Respects a husband’s plea even against his mission, preventing additional trauma for Sun.

Event: Decides against bringing Walt back, believing he’s suffered enough
Explanation: Prioritizes a child’s well-being over his strategic desire to have him on the Island.

Event: Avoids directly telling Walt that Michael died on the freighter
Explanation: Spairs Walt immediate emotional trauma while not outright lying, balancing truth and kindness.

Event: Is opaque about why he believes the Island is special
Explanation: Withholds key explanations, undermining his own credibility and trust within the group.

Event: Lets himself be repeatedly manipulated by Anthony Cooper and Ben due to his need to be special
Explanation: Allows others to exploit him, damaging his life while serving their agendas.

Event: Becomes Jack’s ideological rival and frequently opposes his plans
Explanation: Pursues his faith-based strategy at the expense of Jack’s leadership, sometimes worsening group cohesion.

Event: Tries to lead those who distrust the freighter to the Barracks for safety
Explanation: Acts on a correct suspicion and offers an alternative plan to protect his faction.

Event: Leads his group to the Barracks where mercenaries later attack and kill most of them
Explanation: Outcome shows his “safe” choice actually placed his followers in greater danger.

Event: Warns others that the freighter is not there to rescue them
Explanation: Raises accurate alarm about a hidden threat, giving others a chance (however limited) to prepare.

Event: Fails to convince any of the Oceanic Six to return when he visits them off-island
Explanation: His mission fails and deepens his despair without benefiting them in the short term.

Event: Tracks down Walt years later just to see him, not to recruit him
Explanation: Seeks emotional connection and closure without burdening Walt with Island business.

Event: Goes out of his way early to endear himself: finds Vincent and lets Michael take credit
Explanation: Does a kind act and surrenders credit to strengthen father-son bond.

Event: Helps Charlie overcome heroin addiction
Explanation: Intervenes firmly yet supportively to break Charlie’s self-destructive habit.

Event: Beats Charlie senseless when Charlie’s erratic behavior endangers Claire and Aaron
Explanation: Uses excessive violence to control a danger, protecting others but seriously harming Charlie.

Event: Compels Boone to face his fixation on Shannon
Explanation: Guides Boone into confronting unhealthy attachment, aiding Boone’s growth.

Event: Builds Claire a baby cradle for her birthday
Explanation: Invests effort into a thoughtful, practical gift, strengthening morale and comfort.

Event: Encourages Jack to pursue his “white rabbit” and accept leadership
Explanation: Nudges Jack toward embracing a role he’s suited for, benefiting Jack and the group.

Event: Serves as a mentor to Boone and other survivors, helping them with personal issues
Explanation: Provides guidance and emotional support that fosters resilience and growth.

Event: Administers secret tests of character to his fellow castaways
Explanation: Manipulates others through opaque trials to gauge their faith, risking trust for his own evaluative goals.

Event: Acts as protective “Papa Wolf” toward Claire, Aaron, and Walt
Explanation: Uses his strength and vigilance to shield more vulnerable group members.

Event: Allows Ben to “shoot him and leave him for dead” and later forgives him
Explanation: Repeatedly trusts and forgives a manipulator, hurting himself while enabling Ben and offering moral grace.

Event: Regains ability to walk on the Island and embraces his new role
Explanation: Transforms his life by walking again, then channels that into leadership and hunting.

Event: Becomes a hunter, adventurer, and leader after being a lonely old man in a wheelchair
Explanation: Uses newfound capabilities to gain purpose and materially support the group.

Event: Tries sincerely to protect the Island even when it hurts his relationships
Explanation: Sacrifices social bonds and others’ preferences to serve a grand cause he believes in.

Event: Beats Mikhail severely when Mikhail blocks him from meeting Jacob
Explanation: Inflicts a brutal beatdown to push his own spiritual agenda forward.

Event: Accepts Widmore’s alias “Jeremy Bentham” and travels under it
Explanation: Uses an alias as a practical tool in a complex conflict without clear extra harm or benefit.

Event: Pushes Jack ideologically into faith; after his death, Jack becomes a believer as mirror to Locke
Explanation: His persistent faith-centered opposition ultimately helps Jack grow into a more balanced protector.
